methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $26M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Helios, a Series A climate and energy company founded by Jonathan Geifman and Dor Blum, is valued against a peer universe of 9 private and 1 public comparable across the sector. Total capital raised to date is $26M.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using total raised as a proxy base and applying a 2.0x multiplier typical for Series A advancement, implies a $52M valuation. Peer-median analysis across three same-stage, same-sector comparables—Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M)—yields a median valuation of $150M, with a range of $110M to $200M. The peer median method carries greater weight given direct comparability within the Climate & Energy frontier-tech space.

Triangulating these two methods produces a valuation range of $44M (low) to $172M (high), with a median of $101M. The stage-progression method likely underweights sector momentum reflected in peer valuations, while peer multiples may not fully account for Helios' specific capital efficiency or market position within the cohort. Key uncertainties include revenue traction, technology differentiation relative to Graphyte and Fourth Power, and broader capital market conditions affecting frontier-tech multiples. The wide spread ($128M) between low and high reflects material dispersion in the peer set and method sensitivity.
